5: Arami oved avi vayered Mitzraymah vayagar sham bimtei me'at vayehi sham legoy gadol atzum varav (ʾărammî ʾōbēd ʾābî wayyēred miṣrāymāh wayyāgār šām bimtê mĕʿāṭ wayyĕhî šām lĕgôy gādôl ʿāṣûm wārāb): 'A wandering Aramean was my father, and he went down to Egypt and sojourned there, few in number, and there he became a nation, great, mighty, and populous.' This sentence — the opening of the first-fruits liturgical creed (vv. 5-10) — is Israel's compressed covenant history in confessional form. Arami oved (wandering/perishing Aramean): oved is the participle of abad (to perish, to be lost, to be destroyed), the same root as eved (servant/slave) — though different. The father is Jacob (whose mother was Aramean, and who lived among Arameans); he was one who was about to perish, a non-entity. From this near-extinction, the nation of Israel emerged — the covenant pivot from one man's destitution to a great nation. Paul's doctrine of election (not many wise... not many powerful... God chose what is foolish, 1 Cor 1:26-29) follows the same logic: the covenant community is defined by what it was before YHWH chose it, not by what it was in itself.
17: et-YHWH he'emarta hayom lihyot lecha l'Elohim uve'lekhta bidrachav ve'lishmor chukkav umitzvotav umishpatav velishmo'a beqolo. ve'YHWH he'emircha hayom lihyot lo le'am segulah (ʾet-Yhwh heʾĕmartā hayyôm lihyôt lĕkā lēʾlōhîm ûbĕleket bidĕrākāyw wĕlišmōr ḥuqqāyw ûmiṣwōtāyw ûmišpāṭāyw wĕlišmōaʿ bĕqōlô. wYhwh heʾĕmîrkā hayyôm lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullāh): 'You have caused YHWH to declare today that he will be your God... and YHWH has caused you to declare today that you will be his people of treasured possession.' The Hiphil form heʾĕmartā and heʾĕmîrkā are both causatives of amar (to say/declare) — literally, you have caused him to say, and he has caused you to say. This is covenant ratification language: the two parties publicly commit each other to their respective roles before witnesses. The mutual covenant-declaration structure (Israel declares YHWH as God; YHWH declares Israel as his treasured people) is the OT form of what Paul describes as adoption (Rom 8:15-16: the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry Abba, Father — the Spirit bearing witness with our spirit that we are children of God).
1: veha'ya ki-tavo el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lecha nachalah uvarishtah veyashavta vah (wĕhāyâ kî-tāb̠ōʾ ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lĕkā nĕḥālâ ûb̠ĕrišhtāh wĕyāšab̠tā b̠āh): 'And it shall be when you come into the land that the LORD your God gives you as an inheritance, and you possess it and dwell in it.' The verse frames the first-fruits ritual as a future obligation tied to the gift of land. Nachalah (inheritance, portion): the land is not property acquired but a covenant inheritance granted — the same term used for the tribal allotments and for Israel's own status as YHWH's nachalah (cf. Deut 4:20). Uvarishtah (you possess it) from yarash: to dispossess, to inherit by taking possession. The first-fruits law is prospective — a liturgical response embedded into the act of settlement itself, so that the memory of gift does not erode into an assumption of entitlement.
2: velakachta mereishit kol-pri ha'adamah asher tavi me'artzecha... vesamt batel vehalacht el-hamaqom asher yivchar YHWH Eloheicha leshakken shemo sham (wĕlāqaḥtā mērēʾšît kol-pĕrî hāʾădāmâ... wĕśamtā b̠aṭene wĕhālaḵtā ʾel-hammāqôm ʾăšer yib̠ḥar Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā lĕšakkēn šĕmô šām): Take the first of all the fruit of the ground and put it in a basket, then go to the place that YHWH shall choose to make his name dwell there. Reishit (first, beginning): the first of the harvest is set apart — not because it is better but because it is first, a synecdoche for the whole. Batel (basket, container): the portable vessel for presenting the offering. Hamaqom asher yivchar (the place YHWH shall choose): the centralizing formula of Deuteronomy, pointing to Jerusalem and the Temple. First-fruits are not distributed locally but brought to the singular sanctuary — the spatial corollary of monotheism.
3: uvata el-hakohen asher yihyeh bayamim hahem ve'amarta elav higadeti hayom l'YHWH Eloheicha ki-vati el-ha'aretz asher nishba YHWH la'avoteinu latet lanu (ûb̠āʾtā ʾel-hakkōhēn ʾăšer yihyê b̠ayyāmîm hāhēm wĕʾāmartā ʾēlāyw higgadtî hayyôm lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā kî-b̠āʾtî ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer nišbaʿ Yhwh lāʾăb̠ôtênû lātēt lānû): 'And you shall come to the priest who is in office in those days and say to him, I declare today to the LORD your God that I have come into the land that the LORD swore to our fathers to give us.' Higadeti (I declare, I report, I make known) from nagad: the formal declaration is performative — the gift of land is publicly acknowledged as a fulfilled oath. Nishba (swore): the oath to the patriarchs (Gen 12:7; 13:15; 15:18) has been kept; the worshipper's presence at the sanctuary with first-fruits is its proof.
4: velakach hakohen hatel miyadecha vehinichah lifnei mizbach YHWH Eloheicha (wĕlāqaḥ hakkōhēn haṭṭene miyyāḏĕkā wĕhinnîḥô lip̄nê mizbēaḥ Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'And the priest shall take the basket from your hand and set it down before the altar of the LORD your God.' The priest's action mediates the transfer: the basket moves from the hand of the worshipper to the presence of YHWH at the altar. Hinichah lifnei (set it down before): placement before the altar is the spatial equivalent of presenting it to YHWH himself — the altar as the meeting point of human offering and divine presence. The priest functions here not as a sacrificial agent but as a courier of the worshipper's declaration.
6: vayarei'u otanu haMitzrim vaye'anunu vayitnu aleinu avodah kashah (wayyārēʿû ʾōtānû hammiṣrîm wayyĕʿannûnû wayyitĕnû ʿālênû ʿăb̠ôdâ qāšâ): 'And the Egyptians treated us harshly and afflicted us, and laid on us hard labor.' Part of the recited creed (vv. 5-10). Three verbs of oppression: vayarei'u (made things bad for us, treated us ill — ra'a), vaye'anunu (afflicted us — anah: to humble, to oppress), avodah kashah (hard labor — kasha: harsh, severe). The liturgical creed encodes the memory of bondage so that prosperity never erases the knowledge of what Israel was without YHWH's intervention.
7: vanitztaq el-YHWH Elohei avoteinu vayishma YHWH et-qoleinu vayar et-onyenu ve'et-amaleinu ve'et-lachatzenu (wanniṣʿaq ʾel-Yhwh ʾĕlōhê ʾăb̠ôtênû wayyišmaʿ Yhwh ʾet-qōlênû wayyarʾ ʾet-ʿonyênû wĕʾet-ʿămālênû wĕʾet-laḥăṣênû): 'And we cried to the LORD, the God of our fathers, and the LORD heard our voice, and saw our affliction, our toil, and our oppression.' Three terms for suffering: oni (affliction, humiliation), amal (toil, travail), lachatz (oppression, pressure). The creed emphasizes YHWH's perception — he heard (shama) and saw (ra'ah) — the divine attention that precedes rescue. This forms the theological grammar of the Exodus: cry → YHWH hears and sees → deliverance.
8: vayotzi'enu YHWH miMitzrayim beyad chazakah uvizroa netuyah uvemorah gadol uviotot uvemoftim (wayyôṣiʾênû Yhwh mimmîṣrāyim b̠ĕyād ḥăzāqâ ûb̠izrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ ûb̠emôrāʾ gādôl ûb̠ʾōtôt ûb̠emōp̄tîm): 'And the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with great deeds of terror, with signs and wonders.' The four-fold epitome of the Exodus: yad chazakah (mighty hand), zroa netuyah (outstretched arm), morah gadol (great awe/terror), otot umoftim (signs and wonders). This formula recurs throughout Deuteronomy (4:34; 7:19; 11:2) as the canonical summary of YHWH's redemptive act. The creed performs the Exodus anew each time it is spoken — liturgical memory as identity formation.
9: vayevi'enu el-hamaqom hazeh vayiten lanu et-ha'aretz hazot eretz zavat chalav udvash (wayyĕb̠iʾênû ʾel-hammāqôm hazzê wayyittēn-lānû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ʾereṣ zāb̠at ḥālāb̠ ûd̠ĕb̠aš): 'And he brought us to this place and gave us this land, a land flowing with milk and honey.' The creed reaches its destination: from Aram to Egypt to the wilderness to this place. Eretz zavat chalav udvash (land flowing with milk and honey): the covenant formula first spoken in Exod 3:8 and repeated throughout Deuteronomy. Zavat from zub (to flow): the land is abundant in pastoral and agricultural produce — milk (livestock) and honey (wild honey or date honey, dibs). The creed culminates in gift: YHWH's action ends not in rescue alone but in bestowal.
10: ve'atah hinei heveti et-reishit pri ha'adamah asher-natata li YHWH vehinhachto lifnei YHWH Eloheicha vehishtachavita lifnei YHWH Eloheicha (wĕʿattâ hinnê hēb̠ēʾtî ʾet-rēʾšît pĕrî hāʾădāmâ ʾăšer-nātattā lî Yhwh wĕhinnăḥtô lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā wĕhištaḥăwîtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'And now, behold, I have brought the first of the fruit of the ground, which you, O LORD, have given me. And you shall set it down before the LORD your God and worship before the LORD your God.' The creed pivots to the present act: the historical recitation (vv. 5-9) ends in ve'atah (and now, therefore) — the past determines the present obligation. Hinei heveti (here I bring): the gesture of presentation is the embodied response to the history just recited. Vehishtachavita (and you shall bow down/worship): the presentation culminates in prostration — acknowledgment of lordship.
11: vesimachta bechol-hatov asher natan lecha YHWH Eloheicha ulveitecha atta vehaLevi veha'ger asher beqirbecha (wĕśāmaḥtā b̠ĕḵol-haṭṭôb̠ ʾăšer nātan lĕkā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ûlĕb̠êtĕkā ʾattâ wĕhalLēwî wĕhagēr ʾăšer b̠ĕqirb̠ĕkā): 'And you shall rejoice in all the good that the LORD your God has given to you and to your house, you, and the Levite, and the sojourner who is among you.' Vesimachta (and you shall rejoice): the ritual of first-fruits ends not in solemn duty but in communal joy. Three parties rejoice together: the Israelite householder (atta uveitekha), the Levite (who has no land allotment), and the ger (sojourner/stranger). The economic vulnerability of the Levite and the alien makes their inclusion in covenant joy theologically significant — the gift of land extends its circle beyond those who legally hold it.
12: ki techale la'aser et-kol-ma'aser tevuatecha bashanah hashlishit shenat hama'aser venatata laLevi lager layatom velalmana ve'achlu vishveicha vesave'u (kî tĕḵallê laʿaśēr ʾet-kol-maʿăśar tĕb̠ûʾātĕkā baššānâ haššĕlîšît šĕnat hamma'ăśēr wĕnātattā lalLēwî laggēr lāyyātôm wĕlāʾalmānâ wĕʾāḵĕlû b̠išʿārêkā wĕśāb̠ĕʿû): 'When you have finished paying all the tithe of your produce in the third year, which is the year of tithing, you shall give it to the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, that they may eat within your towns and be filled.' The third-year tithe (ma'aser sheni variant: the triennial tithe for the poor, distinct from the annual tithe consumed at the sanctuary) redirects the offering to local distribution. The four-fold welfare beneficiary list: Levite, ger, yatom (orphan), almanah (widow) — the same quartet of the vulnerable that recurs throughout Deuteronomy (cf. 14:29; 16:11, 14; 24:17-19). The law institutionalizes welfare as a tithe-obligation, not voluntary charity.
13: ve'amarta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha bi'arti haqodesh min-habayit vegam netativ laLevi velager layatom velalmana kechol-mitzvaticha asher tzivitani lo-avarti mimitzvotelcha velo-shachachti (wĕʾāmartā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā biʿartî haqqōḏeš min-hab̠bayit wĕgam nĕtattîw lalLēwî wĕlaggēr layyātôm wĕlāʾalmānâ kĕḵol-miṣwātĕkā ʾăšer ṣiwwîtānî lōʾ-ʿāb̠artî mimmîṣwōtêkā wĕlōʾ šāḵāḥtî): 'Then you shall say before the LORD your God, I have removed the sacred portion (haqodesh) out of my house, and moreover I have given it to the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, according to all your commandment that you have commanded me. I have not transgressed any of your commandments, nor have I forgotten any of them.' The tithe declaration is a covenant compliance report. Bi'arti haqodesh (I have cleared out the sacred portion): bi'er means to remove, clear out, consume entirely — the tithe must be completely distributed, not stored or hoarded. The declaration is first-person, specific, and addressed directly to YHWH — a covenant audit conducted in divine presence.
14: lo-achalti ve'oni mimenu velo-bi'arti mimenu betame'a velo-natati mimenu leMet shamati beqol YHWH Elohai asiti kechol asher tzivitani (lōʾ-ʾāḵaltî b̠ĕʾônî mimmennû wĕlōʾ-b̠iʿartî mimmennû b̠ĕṭāmēʾ wĕlōʾ-nātattî mimmennû lāmēt šāmaʿtî b̠ĕqôl Yhwh ʾĕlōhāy ʿāśîtî kĕḵōl ʾăšer ṣiwwîtānî): 'I have not eaten of the tithe while I was mourning, or removed any of it while I was unclean, or offered any of it to the dead. I have obeyed the voice of the LORD my God. I have done according to all that you have commanded me.' Three negative prohibitions — not eaten in mourning (oni), not handled while ritually impure (tame'), not used it in funerary offerings (laMet, for the dead). Each addresses a specific contamination risk: grief-eating might compromise the joyful character of the offering; ritual impurity would defile a sacred portion; funerary use would blend covenant worship with ancestor cult. The declaration is a formal purity oath.
15: hashkifah mimeon kodshecha min-hashamayim uvarech et-amecha et-Yisrael ve'et ha'adamah asher natata lanu ka'asher nishbata la'avoteinu eretz zavat chalav udvash (hašqîp̄â mimmĕʿôn qoḏšĕkā min-haššāmayim ûb̠āreḵ ʾet-ʿammĕkā ʾet-Yiśrāʾēl wĕʾēt hāʾădāmâ ʾăšer nātattā lānû kāʾăšer nišbaʿtā lāʾăb̠ôtênû ʾereṣ zāb̠at ḥālāb̠ ûd̠ĕb̠aš): 'Look down from your holy habitation, from heaven, and bless your people Israel and the ground that you have given us, as you swore to our fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey.' Hashkifah (look down): the verb shaqaf implies a peering downward from above, the posture of a sovereign surveying his domain. Me'on kodshecha (your holy habitation): heaven itself is YHWH's dwelling — the sanctuary on earth is a copy of the heavenly archetype (cf. Exod 25:40; Heb 9:24). The worshipper who has completed the tithe now appeals from the completed act on earth to YHWH's vantage in heaven — compliance enables intercession.
16: hayom hazeh YHWH Eloheicha metzavecha la'asot et-hachuqqim ha'eleh ve'et-hamishpatim ushemartem va'asitem otam bechol-levavecha uvechol-nafshecha (hayyôm hazzê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā mĕṣawwĕkā laʿăśôt ʾet-haḥuqqîm hāʾēllê wĕʾet-hamišpāṭîm ûšĕmartem waʿăśîtem ʾôtām b̠ĕḵol-lĕb̠āb̠ĕkā ûb̠ĕḵol-nap̄šĕkā): 'This day the LORD your God commands you to do these statutes and rules. You shall therefore be careful to do them with all your heart and with all your soul.' Hayom hazeh (this day): the Deuteronomic emphasis on contemporaneity — the commandment is given not to the wilderness generation alone but to every generation that hears it (cf. 5:3; 29:14). The Shema vocabulary: bechol-levavecha uvechol-nafshecha (with all your heart and with all your soul) — covenant obedience requires total disposition, not merely external compliance.
18: veYHWH he'emircha hayom lihyot lo leam segulah ka'asher diber lach velishmor kol-mitzvotav (wYhwh heʾemîrĕkā hayyôm lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullâ kāʾăšer dib̠ber lāk wĕlišmôr kol-miṣwōtāyw): 'And the LORD has avouched you this day to be a people for his treasured possession, as he has promised you, and to keep all his commandments.' He'emircha: as with v17, the Hiphil of amar means to cause to declare or to declare solemnly — YHWH has formally declared Israel to be his. Am segulah (treasured people, people of special possession): segulah in Akkadian means moveable personal wealth of the king — the private treasure of the sovereign. Israel is not merely YHWH's people but his personal treasure (cf. Exod 19:5; Mal 3:17). The covenant is a mutual declaration of exclusive belonging.
19: ultetecha elyon al kol-hagoyim asher asah litehilah uleShem uletifaret ulihyot am kadosh l'YHWH Eloheicha ka'asher diber (ûlĕtittĕkā ʿelyôn ʿal kol-haggôyîm ʾăšer ʿāśâ litĕhillâ ûlĕšēm ûlĕtip̄ʾāret wĕlihyôt ʿam qāḏôš lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā kāʾăšer dib̠ber): 'And he will set you in praise and in fame and in honor high above all nations that he has made, and that you shall be a people holy to the LORD your God, as he has spoken.' Three-fold elevation: tehilah (praise, renown), shem (name, reputation), tifaret (glory, splendor) — Israel is to be the showcase of YHWH's covenant faithfulness among the nations. Am qadosh (holy people): qadosh (set apart, consecrated) — Israel's holiness is derivative, not intrinsic; it flows from YHWH's sanctifying choice. The NT reapplies this language to the church as the new covenant people (1 Pet 2:9).
